did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as LeNet-5, ResNet-50, VGG-16, AlexNet, and LesionNet-14, as well as Vision Transformers (ViT) and a stacking ensemble model, to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset. The study obtained results by comparing the performance of these AI architectures for skin cancer detection and classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the training and comparison of multiple deep learning models (CNNs, ViT, and a stacking ensemble) on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a methodology section detailing data preprocessing and model architecture, a results section with specific performance metrics (e.g., accuracy, recall, F1-score), and a discussion of findings. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICICIS), it reports new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Towards Reliable Skin Cancer Screening: A Multi-Model Deep Learning Approach on Dermoscopic Images
journal: 2025 2nd International Conference on Integration of Computational Intelligent System (ICICIS)
brief_summary: This study evaluates deep learning models for skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dermoscopic image dataset, which contains 10,015 images across seven diagnostic classes. The researchers compared several architectures, including CNNs (LeNet-5, ResNet-50, VGG-16, AlexNet, LesionNet-14), Vision Transformers (ViT), and a stacking ensemble model, while addressing class imbalance through balanced subsampling and data augmentation. The Vision Transformer achieved the highest individual accuracy of 87.70% and an AUC of 0.90, outperforming other single models. The proposed stacking ensemble model, which aggregated predictions from base models using logistic regression, achieved an accuracy of 85.27%. The results indicate that hybrid approaches leveraging both local feature extraction and global context are promising for reliable, AI-augmented dermatological diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset; noted as a malignant condition with 142 cases in the context of class imbalance limitations.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic classes of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion) from the HAM10000 dataset.
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images. Models (CNNs, ViT, and a stacking ensemble) predict discrete diagnostic categories. The study uses categorical cross-entropy loss and softmax output layers for multi-class classification. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, recall, F1-score, and AUC for distinguishing between cancerous and non-cancerous lesions as well as specific lesion types.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A popular benchmark dataset in dermatological AI studies consisting of 10,015 high-quality dermoscopic images collected from various clinical centers. It covers seven clinically significant lesion classes and reflects true diagnostic difficulties with significant class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training deep learning models (CNNs, ViT) after balanced subsampling (1,119 samples per class) and data augmentation (zoom, rotation, translation, flip). The dataset was split into 80% training and 20% test set with class ratio stratification.
use.testing: Used for testing model performance. The 20% test set split was used for final evaluation. Additionally, a six-fold cross-validation strategy was employed for the stacking ensemble model to ensure unbiased training.
use.other: Used to address class imbalance by augmenting original data with validated samples from the ISIC archive.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure unbiased training for the stacking ensemble model.
Used as a meta-classifier to aggregate predictions from base models in the stacking ensemble.
To evaluate model performance, specifically regarding the detection of minority-class lesions and class imbalance.
To assess the discrimination ability of models, particularly noting that AlexNet and LesionNet-14 had AUC values near 0.5 (random prediction).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, recall, F1-score, and AUC, with comparisons made based on these observed values rather than statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.preprocessing: Balanced subsampling (1,119 samples per class), resizing to 32x32 pixels, normalization to [0, 1], data augmentation (20% zoom, 15° rotations, ±10% translations, horizontal flip)

Vision Transformer.notes: Best performing individual model. Achieved strong generalization and balanced performance across both classes.

Stacking Ensemble.notes: Aggregated predictions from CNN, LeNet5, LesionNet-14, AlexNet, VGG16, and ResNet50 using logistic regression as a meta-classifier. Validated using six-fold cross-validation. Learned most from CNN, followed by LeNet and VGG16; assigned negligible weight to ResNet50, LesionNet-14, and AlexNet.

CNN (Customized).notes: Achieved best AUC among CNNs. Performed well in detecting significant texture and structure information.

LeNet-5.notes: Shallow model produced competitive results when properly adapted.

VGG16.notes: Performance remained biased in favor of the non-cancer class. Weakness in detecting minority-class lesions.

ResNet-50.notes: Performed poorly despite deeper architecture. Indicated that deeper models might not generalize effectively without proper tuning or transfer learning in this environment.

AlexNet.notes: Could not recognize malignant lesions with 0 precision, recall, and F1-score. AUC near 0.5 reflected random pattern of prediction.

LesionNet-14.notes: Could not recognize malignant lesions with 0 precision, recall, and F1-score. AUC near 0.5 reflected random pattern of prediction.

HAM10000.notes: AlexNet and LesionNet-14 showed significantly lower accuracy and failed to detect malignancy. ResNet-50 underperformed relative to simpler architectures like LeNet-5 and the Customized CNN. Vision Transformer outperformed traditional CNN-based models.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-model comparison including Custom CNN, LeNet-5, ResNet-50, VGG-16, AlexNet, LesionNet-14, Vision Transformer (ViT), and a Stacking Ensemble model.
architecture_used.pretraining: Vision Transformer weights were pretrained on large-scale datasets and fine-tuned on the skin cancer data. Other models (CNNs) served as baselines; specific pretraining details for CNNs were not explicitly reported beyond standard architectures.
architecture_used.input_size: 32x32 pixels (images were downscaled from original resolution to speed up training).

architecture_details.custom_cnn: Customized Convolutional Neural Network; performed best among CNNs with AUC 0.90.
architecture_details.vision_transformer: Splits input image into fixed-size patches, embeds each into a token, and feeds to a standard Transformer encoder. Uses self-attention mechanisms for global context and hierarchical token aggregation.
architecture_details.stacking_ensemble: Aggregates predictions from six base models (CNN, LeNet-5, LesionNet-14, AlexNet, VGG-16, ResNet-50) using logistic regression as a meta-classifier.

training_details.activation_functions: ReLU activations used within models; softmax output layers for multi-class classification.
training_details.optimizer: Not explicitly reported for the primary models in the provided text (though Adam is mentioned in literature survey for other studies).
training_details.resampling: Balanced subsampling (1,119 samples per class) to address class imbalance.
training_details.validation_strategy: Six-fold cross-validation used for the stacking ensemble model; 80% training / 20% test split with class ratio stratification for individual models.

classification_or_output_head.type: Softmax output layer for multi-class classification.
classification_or_output_head.classes: Seven diagnostic classes (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion).

preprocessing_techniques: Balanced subsampling (1,119 samples per class) to address class imbalance
Resizing images to 32x32 pixels via bilinear interpolation
Data augmentation: 20% zoom, 15° rotations, ±10% translations, and horizontal flip
Nearest-neighbor interpolation used during augmentation to avoid edge artifacts in lesion structures
80% training and 20% test set split with class ratio stratification

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Horizontal flip applied during real-time augmentation.
Nearest-neighbor interpolation was used during augmentation to avoid edge artifacts in lesion structures.

conclusions: Vision Transformers (ViT) outperformed traditional Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) in skin lesion classification, achieving the highest accuracy of 87.70%, an AUC of 0.90, and a recall of 0.82 for the cancerous class, demonstrating strong generalization and balanced performance.

The proposed stacking ensemble model, which aggregated predictions from CNN, LeNet-5, LesionNet-14, AlexNet, VGG16, and ResNet-50 using logistic regression, achieved an accuracy of 85.27%, effectively leveraging the complementary strengths of CNNs for local texture extraction and Transformers for global context.

AlexNet and LesionNet-14 failed to recognize malignant lesions, showing 0 precision, recall, and F1-score for the malignancy class and AUC values near 0.5, indicating random prediction patterns despite an overall accuracy of approximately 80%.
VGG16 performance was biased toward the non-cancer class, with a low recall of 0.34 and F1-score of 0.44 for cancer cases, highlighting weaknesses in detecting minority-class lesions.

ResNet-50 underperformed relative to simpler architectures like LeNet-5 and the Customized CNN, suggesting that deeper models may not generalize effectively without proper tuning or transfer learning in this specific context.
Simpler architectures, when well-trained and optimized, can perform better than more complex ones in certain clinical image classification tasks.
The study identified class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset as a primary challenge, noting that while resampling helped, advanced strategies are needed for extremely rare cancer subtypes.
Downscaling images to 32x32 pixels accelerated training but risked discarding fine diagnostic details, suggesting adaptive resolution methods as a future research direction.
The models struggled with lesions having unfamiliar appearances or artifacts like hair occlusion, a limitation also faced by experienced dermatologists.

Future work should focus on multimodal data integration (e.g., patient history, genetic risk), improving model interpretability, ensuring diverse training sets to address missing skin tones, and conducting real-world clinical validation.
important_remarks: The study relies on the HAM10000 dataset, which has known geographic and demographic biases and significant class imbalance (e.g., only 142 melanoma cases), limiting external validity.
Images were downsampled to 32x32 pixels to accelerate training, a decision that risks discarding fine diagnostic details critical for dermatological assessment.

AlexNet and LesionNet-14 failed completely to detect malignancy (0 precision, recall, and F1-score for the malignancy class), despite reporting ~80% overall accuracy, highlighting the danger of relying solely on accuracy metrics in imbalanced datasets.

ResNet-50 underperformed (AUC 0.66) compared to simpler architectures like LeNet-5, suggesting that deeper models may not generalize effectively without specific tuning or transfer learning in this low-resolution context.
The stacking ensemble model assigned negligible or no weight to ResNet-50, LesionNet-14, and AlexNet, indicating these base models contributed minimally to the final prediction.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; performance comparisons are based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, F1-score).

The study addresses skin tone bias by augmenting data with validated samples from the ISIC archive but acknowledges that missing skin tones and rare lesion subtypes remain challenges for generalizability.
Models struggled with lesions having unfamiliar appearances or artifacts like hair occlusion, a limitation shared by experienced dermatologists.
The paper does not report external validation on independent datasets, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical deployment readiness.
